text: Anything to help give people the proper sense of scale.

Coltrane ranges from super mellow to unbearable atonality/harshness. The album he did with Johnny Hartman is one of the most mellow records you will ever hear. There is also a compilation called the "gentle side of JC" that is particularly pleasant. His wife Alice Coltrane was no slouch either, and she made a lot of spacey harp music at the same time as he was doing his stuff. Coltrane got into heroin and then kicked it so there an intensity in his work, depending on his physical state, that is quite extreme. And of course you are getting McCoy Tyner who is super great. Tyner still performs a bit, he is one of the only survivors of that era. sorry i misspelled Durufle earlier. He wrote very little music, but his Requiem i believe is even nicer than Faure's. And also worth checking out is Cannonball Adderly & Nancy Wilson. She was just a kid at the time, her first record, and it is spectacular. Youth is so beautiful.

The LIVE Programming Workshop call for submissions is up: <https://2019.splashcon.org/home/live-2019#Call-for-Papers-Essays-and-Demos>

Good (best?) meeting place for the interests of this community

attachments.title: LIVE 19  - SPLASH 2019
attachments.text: The LIVE’19 workshop invites submissions of ideas for improving programming via liveness. Live programming gives the programmer immediate feedback on the behavior of a program as it is edited, replacing the edit-compile-debug cycle with a fluid programming experience. The most commonly used live programming environment is the spreadsheet, but there are many others. The study of live programming is now a [re-]established area of research. This year we would like to reflect on what has been achieved, what has been learnt, and what remains to be done, growing up from a nascent community into ...

<@UGMH4H202> They're both good. LIVE is more focused on programming per se. PPIG has a broader interest in the human side of computing: programming, using, and even politics. But despite that broadness it feels like a closer-knit community with deeper commitments.

Hi everyone. I'm proud to release v0.0.1 of Mech today, the first of many. It's more of a test-run to figure out how releases will work, so documentation will come with the next release v0.0.2. Still, I figured some people here might be interested to read a blog post and play with a little robot arm I wrote in Mech. Let me know any feedback you have! <http://mech-lang.org> and try it out online here <http://try.mech-lang.org|try.mech-lang.org>

i see your clock program. Not too far from Beads so can't be too critical hah. I do however think that the usage of [# space] to be a comment and [## space] to be a higher level comment is understandable, but having # prefix for an identifier to mean a tracked variable seems ill-advised. Why not use one of the other dozen punctuation characters like @ or % as a significant prefix. Seems way too dangerous to overload the comment delimiter with a functional prefix, when the only difference is a small space character. Makes it much harder to read; you are trying to train the eye to skip over the comments quickly and this will impede that training. So my criticism is about ergonomics. This is one of the things i hate about languages which overload the + operator. So plus can mean in some languages vector addition, string concatenation, all sorts of stuff. In FORTH you could redefine the + operator which was insane.

I like # for tables for the same reason we had them in Eve, because they have similar semantics to a # in Twitter, meaning a collection of things. I'm going to use a @ in the future to mean a specific row, which again will have semantics similar to Twitter

i now have clock, wristwatch, tic-tac-toe, snake, chess, networked tic tac toe, and planning some for engineering use that employ lots of physical units, and then some for business that do your classic sum of territories sales report or stock portfolio.  Controlling a robot is a great idea. Sure wish Sony wasn't holding back on the Aibo; there isn't a commercial robot that is nimble that is open source yet to my knowledge. Huge mistake on Sony's part not to open it up to 3rd parties fully.

I am sure you have seen the boston dynamics stuff, but what we need is a consumer friendly priced robot chassis with enough quick motors and sensors to be be feasible, and then let people figure out how to make it do something useful.

I'm planning on using Mech to control one of these: <https://www.sphero.com/sphero-bolt>

My goal is to get like 2 dozen and use them for a summer program for middle schoolers. We currently have a program at Lehigh where we use Matlab and iRobot creates. I think Mech and spheros would be a good upgrade

Hello, World! I'm Wouter van Oortmerssen and I've made a few programming languages (<http://strlen.com/programming-languages/>) probably the most futuristic being Aardappel (graphical distributed tree rewriting by example, <http://strlen.com/aardappel-language/>) and Restructor (fully automatic whole program refactoring, also graphical, <http://strlen.com/restructor/>). My main project right now is a more conventional language, Lobster, that nevertheless has some innovation (e.g. flow sensitive type specialization, automatic lifetime analysis, FRP-like frame variables etc, <http://strlen.com/lobster/)>. On the sillier side I once caused an esolang revival with <http://strlen.com/false-language/>. Oh and in my day job I work on LLVM and WebAssembly :slightly_smiling_face: Also make games and game engines, and productivity tools like TreeSheets. Details: <http://strlen.com/>
